title: Bird Tree Morning
desc: At dawn, even Gloomuck Swamp has a way of making creatures stop and look.

steps.text:
At dawn, Gloomuck Swamp is briefly gentler than it has any right to be. The stink is still there, the mud still breathes in slow foul bubbles, but a pale silver light lies over the water and the tall reeds hold it the way cleaner places hold mist. Out in the middle distance, the famous bird-shaped tree rises above the muck with its long branch-neck and hooked crown, ridiculous and stately at once.

A marsh guide waits on the root walk with a coil of fresh ribbons and a satchel of polished shell discs. "Morning circuit," she says. "If the markers stay good, the swamp is only unpleasant. If they fail, it gets creative." Around her, frogs call from somewhere unseen and a line of blue insects drifts low over the water like moving sparks.

You can retie the channel ribbons along the root path toward the bird tree, or climb the lookout perch and clean the shell reflectors that catch early light for travelers coming in from the firmer ground.

options.label: Retie the channel ribbons along the root path
Climb the perch and set the shell reflectors

steps.text:
The root path rides just above the worst of the muck, twisting from hummock to hummock where old growth decided not to surrender. You tie fresh ribbons to low branches and jutting roots, replacing the damp faded strips with clean colors that show up even through morning haze.

As you work farther in, the swamp opens by degrees. Black water reflects the sky in broken pieces. Mats of green scum tremble where something beneath them passes slowly by. The bird tree grows larger with each marker until its silhouette stops looking absurd and starts looking dependable, the one elegant landmark in a landscape devoted to bad decisions.

By the time you reach the final knot point, the route behind you reads clearly from one rise to the next. The guide looks back along the line of ribbons and seems pleased in a private, professional way. "Good," she says. "Now the beauty will not distract anyone into drowning before breakfast."

options.label: Stand a moment and take in the dawn

steps.text:
The lookout perch is lashed to the trunk of a half-dead tree at the swamp's edge, high enough to catch the first useful light and low enough to remind you what would happen if the lashings were not checked. The shell discs are mounted in simple frames, angled to reflect dawn toward the safest approach line.

You wipe each shell clean of film and set it back into position while the guide watches how the pale light jumps from one reflector to the next. Once aligned, the discs send a faint sequence of bright flashes over the reeds, not enough to look theatrical, just enough to tell an incoming traveler where the firmest ground begins.

From the perch, Gloomuck is ugly in a way that loops all the way back to grandeur. Water, mist, sulfur stain, black roots, the long absurd silhouette of the bird tree, all of it spreads outward in layers of marsh color that no one would call clean and no one who has seen it forgets. The guide notices you looking. "I said it was unpleasant," she says. "I did not say it was ordinary."

options.label: Climb down with the shells shining
